abstract.text: A Swift engine for writing parser-driven interactive fiction — text adventures in the tradition of Zork and Infocom’s ZIL.

inlineContent.text: Gnusto turns a single Swift type into a playable text adventure. You
your world — its rooms, its things, and the rules that govern them — and the engine parses player input, runs the turn, and prints the result.

code: let intro = "You wake in a small, bright room."
description("A plain white room with a single door, to the north.")

so the calling rule can embellish the result — print something more, read
state the default action changed, and so on.

A Swift engine for writing parser-driven interactive fiction — text adventures in the tradition of Zork and Infocom’s ZIL.
The game world: owns all state, serializes all mutation, and runs the
turn pipeline. The single
in a game sits between the REPL and
this actor.
How a single Swift type becomes a world the engine can run.
Give a region its own declarations — its own type, and even its own package.
A reusable game system — commerce, combat, dialog, magic — packaged as an
importable unit of
and
, that a host game opts into.
Custom game state with the same ergonomics as built-in state:
Alternative nouns the parser accepts for the item.
The maximum number of items that may be placed directly inside a container
(enforced by the put-in action).
A container’s contents are visible even while it is closed (but still not
reachable until it is opened) — a glass jar, a display case.
A uniform draw from the range, for rule bodies:
A replacement or new stage-4 default action for one intent — the seam
that lets a game or plugin give a custom verb real behavior instead of
“I didn’t understand”, or replace a built-in’s default entirely.
The display name of a location. The last word becomes a parser noun.
One statement in a
block: an exit, a blocked exit, an initial item
placement, or the player’s starting location.
Feeds a fixed list of commands and records everything into a transcript —
for tests and automated playthroughs. Ships in the library because game
authors want transcript tests too.
